chunk_summary: This section outlines protocols for contamination investigation and product recall in human milk banks, emphasizing quarantine, traceability, corrective actions, and structured RCA/CAPA plans to ensure safety and quality of donor human milk (DHM).
learning_objectives: Understand the steps for managing contamination events in human milk banks, including quarantine and traceability.
Learn the criteria for identifying and classifying contamination events in milk banks.
Develop skills in executing root cause analysis (RCA) and corrective and preventive actions (CAPA) for process improvement.
Recognize the importance of communication with families and NICU teams during contamination events.
key_facts: Contamination triggers include post-pasteurization culture growth above acceptance thresholds, repeated high pre-pasteurization bioburden, and pasteurizer time–temperature deviations.
Immediate action involves quarantining affected inventory and halting further dispensing until the issue is clarified.
Culture-positive post-pasteurization batches are discarded; repasteurization is generally avoided due to its potential to mask process defects.
Trace-and-recall procedures require identifying all recipients of affected DHM using batch-to-bottle-to-recipient logs.
Structured RCA and CAPA plans include assessing aseptic techniques, equipment calibration, and cold-chain practices before resuming operations.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: A contamination event in a human milk bank should be managed like a tissue/blood product deviation, with rapid quarantine, traceability-based recipient identification, and documented corrective actions.
document_key_topics: Contamination triggers and investigation in milk banks
Quarantine and traceability protocols for DHM
Root cause analysis and corrective actions
Communication with NICU teams and families
